pattern: Grow two disjoint-set forests (one per traveler) greedily, spending shared edges first since a shared edge can satisfy both forests for the price of one.
hints: An edge is only worth keeping if it merges two components that weren't already connected for whichever traveler(s) can use it.
Process the both-travelers edges before either traveler's private edges — a shared edge that merges components is strictly more valuable than two private edges doing the same job.

Keep one union-find per traveler; a shared edge counts as used if it merges components in either structure, then finish each traveler's spanning forest with their own private edges; both forests must end as a single component or the answer is -1.

edge_inputs.description: a redundant cycle built entirely from shared edges
a shared backbone plus needed private edges plus one truly redundant private edge
Alice's edges leave a node unreachable, so the graph can never be fully traversable for her
minimal two-node graph with a single shared edge that cannot be removed
an exact duplicate shared edge contributes nothing beyond the first copy
only private edges, both travelers need every one of their own edges
one traveler's private edges alone already connect everything while the other relies on shared edges

stress.description: a random shared-edge spanning backbone (so the graph is always feasible) plus a pile of random extra edges of mixed types
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = max(2, min(size, 200))
    order = list(range(1, n + 1))
    rng.shuffle(order)
    edges = []
    for i in range(1, n):
        u = order[i]
        v = order[rng.randint(0, i - 1)]
        edges.append([3, u, v])
    extra = min(300, max(1, size // 10))
    for _ in range(extra):
        u = rng.randint(1, n)
        v = rng.randint(1, n)
        if u == v:
            continue
        edges.append([rng.randint(1, 3), u, v])
    return [n, edges]
stress.note: a random spanning tree of type-3 edges guarantees the answer is never -1, so the stress focuses on counting used edges correctly
